question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `filter(e -> e.getType() == type)` post-query step in `getByAliasAndType()` during a network partition where the database returns a partial/corrupted entity object, causing a `NullPointerException` or `ClassCastException` in the lambda that is not caught by `EntityServiceImpl`, resulting in an unhandled exception that crashes the request and leaks internal details?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of any rate limiting in `EntityServiceImpl.getByIdAndType()` to send a request storm during a network partition recovery phase, overwhelming the database with reconnection attempts and queries as the partition heals, preventing the service from recovering and extending the outage beyond the partition duration?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `EntityServiceImpl` has no bulkhead isolation between `getByIdAndType`, `getByAliasAndType`, and `getByEvmAddressAndType` to cause a network partition affecting only one query path to exhaust shared thread pool resources, degrading all three entity lookup methods simultaneously even though only one database query type is affected?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shared `entityRepository` instance in `EntityServiceImpl` — injected via `@RequiredArgsConstructor` — to cause a network partition affecting `findByAlias()` to also degrade `findById()` and `findByEvmAddress()` calls, since all three methods share the same JPA connection pool, creating a single point of failure for all entity resolution paths?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a health check bypass in `EntityServiceImpl` during a network partition to cause the Kubernetes/load balancer liveness probe to fail (since it also hits the database), triggering pod restarts that interrupt in-flight requests and create a restart loop that extends the service outage beyond the partition duration?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the two-step database access pattern in `getByEvmAddressAndType()` — where a long-zero address calls `findById` and a non-zero address calls `findByEvmAddress` — during a network partition where only one of these query paths is affected, to probe which database query type is partitioned by observing differential response times, leaking infrastructure topology information?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the synchronous blocking nature of `entityRepository.findById()` in `getByIdAndType()` during a network partition to cause the reactive/async GraphQL request handling layer to block its worker threads, violating the non-blocking contract of the framework and causing cascading failures in unrelated GraphQL resolvers that share the same thread pool?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `getByAliasAndType()` calls `decodeBase32(alias)` synchronously before the database call, and during a network partition, the decoded bytes are held in memory for the entire duration of the blocked `findByAlias()` call, causing memory accumulation proportional to the number of concurrent blocked requests and the size of the alias input?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `ByteBuffer.wrap(evmAddressBytes)` allocation in `getByEvmAddressAndType()` during a network partition to cause each blocked request to hold a `ByteBuffer` object in memory for the entire duration of the partition, contributing to heap pressure when thousands of requests are blocked simultaneously?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of a fallback response (e.g., cached last-known-good result) in `getByIdAndType()` during a network partition to cause the GraphQL API to return errors for all entity ID lookups, breaking dependent client applications that have no retry logic and treating the error as a permanent entity-not-found condition?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the native SQL query in `findByAlias()` — `select * from entity where alias = ?1 and deleted is not true` — during a network partition to cause the database driver to hold a prepared statement handle open indefinitely, consuming server-side cursor resources on the database and preventing other connections from acquiring statement handles after the partition heals?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the native SQL query in `findByEvmAddress()` — `select * from entity where evm_address = ?1 and deleted is not true` — during a network partition to cause the JDBC driver to block on socket read with no timeout, holding a database connection in a `CLOSE_WAIT` TCP state that is never cleaned up, permanently reducing the available connection pool size?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Spring Data JPA `CrudRepository.findById()` call in `getByIdAndType()` during a network partition to trigger a `TransactionSystemException` that wraps a `JDBCConnectionException`, and if this exception is not properly sanitized by the GraphQL error handler, expose the full JDBC connection string including credentials in the error response?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a `@Transactional(timeout=N)` annotation on `getByAliasAndType()` to cause the Spring transaction manager to never time out the `findByAlias()` call during a network partition, allowing the transaction to remain open indefinitely and holding database locks that block write operations on the entity table?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a `@Transactional(timeout=N)` annotation on `getByEvmAddressAndType()` to cause the Spring transaction manager to never time out the `findByEvmAddress()` or `findById()` call during a network partition, allowing transactions to accumulate and exhaust the database's maximum concurrent transaction limit?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of a `@Transactional(readOnly=true)` annotation on `getByIdAndType()` to cause the database to acquire unnecessary write locks during a network partition recovery, where the database is processing a backlog of queries, increasing contention and slowing recovery?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `EntityServiceImpl` is a `@Named` singleton bean with a shared `entityRepository` reference to cause a network partition affecting `getByAliasAndType()` to corrupt the shared state of the `EntityRepository` proxy object, causing subsequent calls from other users to also fail even after the partition heals?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `buffer.getInt() == 0 && buffer.getLong() == 0` short-circuit evaluation in `getByEvmAddressAndType()` during a network partition to cause the service to always take the `findById` path (by sending long-zero addresses), which may have different timeout behavior than `findByEvmAddress`, allowing the attacker to selectively target the slower-failing code path?
